summary:
Lahuradewa is a Neolithic site in Sant Kabir Nagar district of Uttar Pradesh, northern India, dated by radiocarbon to approximately 7000 to 6000 BCE, making it one of the oldest known Neolithic sites in the Indian subcontinent and one of the earliest sites with evidence of rice cultivation in South Asia. Excavated by an Indian team beginning in 2001, the site has challenged earlier assumptions about the origins of South Asian agriculture and generated significant scholarly debate.

The plant remains from Lahuradewa include both wild and possibly domesticated or proto-domesticated rice, as well as evidence of other cultivated or managed plants. The site's dates, if the earliest radiocarbon estimates are accepted, would make Lahuradewa contemporaneous with or even earlier than the known centers of rice domestication in the Yangtze River valley of China, raising the possibility of an independent South Asian center of rice cultivation — though this interpretation remains debated. The associated ceramics and stone tools are relatively simple, reflecting an early stage of Neolithic development.

Lahuradewa is significant both for what it suggests about the depth of South Asian agricultural prehistory and as an example of how archaeological research in regions outside the traditional heartlands of prehistoric study continues to revise and complicate the global story of the Neolithic transition. It contributes to a growing recognition that agricultural origins were more geographically complex and regionally varied than earlier models had proposed.
